1.synthesis: Word reaches Jesus that Lazarus — "he whom you love" (v.3) — is sick. Jesus declares the illness is "for the glory of God, so that the Son of God may be glorified through it" (v.4). The Evangelist notes his love for the family, then notes the two-day delay (vv.5–6). When Jesus proposes returning to Judea, the disciples recall the recent stoning attempts; he answers with the twelve-hour day saying — the one who walks in the light does not stumble (vv.9–10). Then plainly: "Lazarus has died, and for your sake I am glad I was not there, so that you may believe" (vv.14–15). Thomas, with grim loyalty, says: "Let us also go, that we may die with him" (v.16).
1.historical_context: Bethany lay two miles from Jerusalem on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, making it both a natural pilgrimage staging point and uncomfortably close to the authorities who had plotted Jesus's arrest (John 11:53). The two-day delay is theologically purposeful: later rabbinic tradition held that the soul hovered near the body for three days, giving hope of re-entry, but departed definitively on the fourth day when decomposition was visible. Four days would mean unambiguous, unrecoverable death (v.17, v.39). The twelve-hour-day saying (vv.9–10) operates within the Jewish reckoning of daylight hours (twelve equal divisions from sunrise to sunset), with the theological freight that walking in one's appointed light prevents stumbling. Thomas's remark "let us also go, that we may die with him" (v.16) reflects the patron-client code of loyalty: a true philos does not abandon the patron in danger, even at personal cost.
1.literary_structure: The section operates on multiple levels of irony. Jesus's love for Lazarus is stated (v.5) immediately before the delay that will allow his death — the Evangelist forces the reader to hold love and apparent inaction in tension before the resolution arrives. The disciples' fear (v.8) frames the journey to Judea as a march toward death; Jesus reframes it as walking in the light with work to complete. The sleep/death misunderstanding (vv.11–13) is the chapter's opening instance of Johannine misunderstanding: the disciples are relieved by the wrong word. "So that you may believe" (v.15) identifies the disciples as the primary intended recipients of the sign — not merely the mourning community, but the Twelve who will carry the message of the resurrection throughout the world.
1.theological_application: The delay of the one who loves is one of the deepest patterns in the spiritual life. The Evangelist has constructed this scene so that the delay is not a failure of love but its instrument — Jesus allows the situation to reach a point beyond human hope so that the sign will ground faith on something larger than a healing. Every reader who has prayed and waited in silence must sit with the fact that the God who loves does not always act on the timetable that love would seem to require. The disciples are invited not into resignation but into a faith that can sustain the delay: "so that you may believe." Thomas's courage is also here — a kind of love that does not demand safety as a condition, even when it cannot yet see how the story ends.
1.christology: "This illness... is for the glory of God, so that the Son of God may be glorified through it" (v.4) introduces the key Johannine connection between sign and glory: the greatest sign of the public ministry will be the most direct disclosure of who Jesus is, and it will lead directly to the cross. In John's Gospel, the glory of God and the glory of the Son are inseparable (John 17:1): the Father is glorified in the Son's works, and the Son's glorification includes both the signs and the passion. The claim to know that Lazarus has died without a messenger (v.14) is one more marker of Jesus's unique access to the Father's knowledge — a knowledge that governs his movements on a timetable others cannot see.

17.pericope_label: I Am the Resurrection and the Life
17.synthesis: Jesus arrives to find Lazarus four days entombed. Martha goes out to meet him and speaks the shared lament of both sisters: "Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died" (v.21). She reaches beyond grief toward a hedged hope: "even now God will give you whatever you ask" (v.22). Jesus tells her Lazarus will rise; she understands this as the last-day resurrection she already believes in (v.24). He corrects her frame: "I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live, and everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die. Do you believe this?" (vv.25–26). Her answer is the Gospel's confessional summit: "Lord, I believe that you are the Christ, the Son of God, who is coming into the world" (v.27). Mary meets him weeping; the crowd follows weeping; and Jesus, "deeply moved in his spirit and greatly troubled," weeps (vv.33–35).
17.historical_context: Martha's meeting of Jesus outside the village reflects both the mourning conventions and social mobility of a household managing public condolence: the senior sister could leave the shiva house to greet an arriving patron. The "many Jews" from Jerusalem (v.19) performing nihum aveilim (comforting mourners) reflect the communal obligation and signal the family's high social standing. Martha's confession at v.27 — "the Christ, the Son of God, who is coming into the world" — uses the vocabulary of the Gospel's stated purpose (John 20:31) and parallels the Synoptic Petrine confession (Matthew 16:16), here spoken by a woman at a graveside. Jesus's emotional response — the verb embrimaomai (v.33, v.38) typically denotes indignation or the snorting of horses — suggests something more charged than sorrow: righteous anger at death itself, or at the unbelief surrounding him, mingled with genuine grief. "Jesus wept" (v.35) uses edakrysen (quiet tears) rather than the wailing (eklaion) of Mary and the crowd — a distinction the Evangelist preserves.
17.literary_structure: The section is constructed as a series of encounters that escalate Christological disclosure. Martha's conversation produces the "I am the resurrection" declaration and her full confession; Mary's arrival brings grief and communal weeping that draws Jesus into the human experience of death with no dialogue at all — just tears. The crowd's divided response anticipates the chapter's close: "Could not he who opened the eyes of the blind man have kept this man from dying?" (v.37) returns to the Bethesda/Siloam healing as the measure of Jesus's power, now found apparently insufficient. The Evangelist arrays the emotional responses of Martha (faith-reaching-through-grief), Mary (overwhelmed weeping), the crowd (sympathetic puzzlement), and Jesus (moved, troubled, weeping) to show that the resurrection the chapter promises is not won from the outside but from the depths of the shared human experience of loss.
17.theological_application: "I am the resurrection and the life" (v.25) collapses Martha's future-oriented faith into a present-tense reality. She believed correctly in the last-day resurrection; Jesus reveals that the last day is not a date on a calendar but a person standing in front of her. The question "Do you believe this?" (v.26) is the Gospel's sharpest personal challenge: not "do you believe in the doctrine?" but "do you believe in me?" — the one who is the resurrection itself. The weeping of Jesus (v.35) guards against a docetism that would see divine impassibility above the suffering world; the one who will raise the dead also weeps at the graveside, and his tears are not a concession to human weakness but the full participation in creaturely grief that makes the resurrection credible as an act of love.
17.christology: "I am the resurrection and the life" is the fifth of the seven "I am" sayings with a predicate in John. It differs from the others in one crucial respect: it does not say Jesus gives or provides resurrection life but that he is it. Martha's correct eschatological belief pointed to an event; Jesus points to himself as the personal ground of that event. The resurrection is not a divine mechanism that operates independently; it is an expression of who Jesus is. This is why Lazarus's raising is not presented as a mere foreshadowing but as the sign of signs: in it, the one who will be raised on the third day demonstrates his authority over death by giving life to another. The tears at the graveside are not a contradiction of the power about to be displayed but its human face — the God who raises the dead does so from inside the grief of death, not from a safe distance above it.

38.synthesis: Jesus comes to the tomb — a cave with a stone laid against it. Again "deeply moved" (v.38), he orders the stone removed. Martha objects: "by this time there is a bad odor, for he has been there four days" (v.39). Jesus reminds her: "Did I not tell you that if you believed you would see the glory of God?" (v.40). The stone is taken away. Jesus lifts his eyes and prays aloud — "I thank you that you have heard me... I said this on account of the people standing around, that they may believe that you sent me" (vv.41–42). Then: "Lazarus, come out!" (v.43). The dead man comes out, bound in grave-cloths, and Jesus says: "Unbind him, and let him go" (v.44).
38.historical_context: The cave tomb sealed with a stone is the standard Second Temple burial type found throughout the Jerusalem hills: a rock-cut chamber entered through a low opening blocked by a flat slab or rolling disk-stone. Jewish burial involved washing the body, anointing it with aromatic preparations (myrrh, aloes), and wrapping it in linen strips (keiriai) with a separate cloth (soudarion) around the face and head. Without embalming, decomposition in the Palestinian climate would be well advanced by four days — Martha's objection is physically accurate. Jesus's public prayer is explicitly pedagogical: "I said this on account of the people standing around" (v.42), making the sign a transparent act of testimony. The command to unbind the grave-cloths is addressed to the bystanders, involving the community in the restoration — a detail that contrasts pointedly with the empty grave-cloths left behind at the resurrection, where no one unwraps anything (John 20:6–7).
38.literary_structure: The raising itself is narrated with deliberate compression. No thunder, no elaborate ritual, no extended prayer for divine power — just a single imperative addressed by name to a specific dead man: "Lazarus, come out!" Later Christian reflection (Chrysostom, Augustine) noted that the name is important: without it, all the graves might have responded. The command and its fulfillment are juxtaposed without pause; the Evangelist gives no description of what happens inside the tomb. The community's role — removing the stone, unbinding the man — frames the miracle as an act in which the community participates both before and after. The contrast with Jesus's own resurrection is carefully set up: Lazarus is raised still bound and is released by others; Jesus leaves his wrappings folded in the tomb and exits without assistance (John 20:6–7).
38.theological_application: The prayer before the miracle is one of the most instructive moments in the Gospel for understanding how Jesus relates to the Father. He does not pray to receive power he lacks; he prays to make visible the unity and trust that already exist. "I knew that you always hear me" (v.42) is the confession of an unbroken relationship, not a petition for something uncertain. The public prayer is a gift to the witnesses — they are being invited to see what the relationship between Father and Son looks like from the inside. The unbinding command — "let him go" — is the restoration of Lazarus to ordinary human life, but it also carries the weight of the whole Gospel's purpose: what death binds, Christ releases.
38.christology: "Lazarus, come out!" is the most concise display of divine authority in the Gospel. Death is addressed as a condition that can be commanded to release what it holds; the personal name is the sign that the command is particular, not generic. The authority to call the dead by name and have them obey belongs, in Jewish theology, exclusively to God: "I put to death and I bring to life" (Deuteronomy 32:39). The connection to the Shepherd discourse is exact: "the sheep hear his voice... he calls his own sheep by name and leads them out" (John 10:3). Lazarus, dead and sealed in stone, hears the shepherd's voice and comes out. This is what the I AM saying of John 11:25 looks like when it acts: the resurrection is not a doctrine but a person who speaks, and the dead obey him.

45.synthesis: Many who witnessed the raising believe (v.45); some report to the Pharisees (v.46). The chief priests and Pharisees convene the Sanhedrin: "What are we to do? For this man performs many signs. If we let him go on like this, everyone will believe in him, and the Romans will come and take away both our place and our nation" (vv.47–48). Caiaphas cuts through: "It is better for you that one man should die for the people, not that the whole nation should perish" (v.50). The Evangelist pauses to note this was prophecy — "that Jesus would die for the nation, and not for the nation only, but also to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad" (vv.51–52). From that day the decision is made. Jesus withdraws to Ephraim; the Passover approaches; people wonder whether he will come to the feast (vv.54–56).
45.historical_context: The Sanhedrin was a body of 71 members — chief priests (predominantly Sadducean), elders, and scribes — that met under the high priest's presidency and exercised broad judicial and religious authority in Jerusalem. Caiaphas served as high priest ca. 18–36 CE, an unusually long tenure reflecting deep Roman confidence. His political calculus — sacrifice one to preserve the many — was a form of colonial realism familiar from Josephus's accounts of how the Jerusalem elite navigated Roman power. The village of Ephraim (v.54) is generally identified with modern et-Taiyibeh, approximately 15 miles northeast of Jerusalem in the Judean desert, just outside the immediate reach of the Sanhedrin. The pre-Passover purification period (v.55) brought pilgrims to Jerusalem a week or two early to complete the seven-day cleansing required for those who had contracted corpse-impurity (Numbers 19:11–20).
45.literary_structure: The section is structured as the Gospel's structural hinge: the greatest sign leads directly to the formal death decision. The Sanhedrin's fear — that the signs will draw everyone to Jesus and bring Rome down — frames belief as politically dangerous and the cross as politically necessary. Caiaphas's utilitarian statement is presented as simultaneously a cynical political calculation and, in the Evangelist's interpretive aside (vv.51–52), an unwitting prophecy: the high priest speaks more truly than he knows, and the scope of the truth surpasses his intention (not the nation only but all the scattered children of God). The reference to the high-priestly office as a vehicle of involuntary prophecy (v.51) draws on a Jewish tradition about the prophetic capacity inherent in the office. Jesus's withdrawal and the Passover crowd's anxious question close the chapter on the threshold of the passion narrative.
45.theological_application: Caiaphas's logic — one man for the many — is the logic of every scapegoat mechanism, and the Evangelist transforms it into gospel without endorsing its cynicism: the one man does die for the people, and the nation is thereby not destroyed but redeemed. The mechanism of sacrifice that Caiaphas proposes as expediency is received by the Evangelist as the pattern of atonement. The scatter-gathering promise of v.52 — children of God dispersed across the world brought into one by the death of Jesus — is the universal scope of the cross: not a Jewish political solution but a cosmic reconciliation. The Passover context deepens the irony further: the lamb that prevents destruction is about to be prepared.
45.christology: The Evangelist's comment that Caiaphas "prophesied" because he was high priest that year (v.51) draws on the Jewish tradition of high-priestly prophetic capacity (Josephus records that John Hyrcanus prophesied while serving as high priest), but the Evangelist's use of it is ironic: the office that should have recognized the Messiah instead sentences him to death, yet in doing so utters the truth of the atonement. The death of Jesus gathers into one the dispersed children of God (v.52) — the "other sheep" of John 10:16, the Gentile God-fearers, all who have been scattered by sin and exile. The cross does not merely prevent the loss that Caiaphas feared; it achieves the ingathering that Isaiah promised (Isaiah 11:12; Isaiah 43:5–6) — and that ingathering is accomplished not by force but by the death of the shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep.
